claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me start by asking you about your child. 

**To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to know:**

**How old is the child you're claiming for?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are generally not eligible

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have more than one child you'd like to claim for, let me know about all of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit for my two children. 

My oldest, Alex, is 10 years old, and my youngest, Blake, is 2 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you with your Child Benefit claim for Alex (10 years old) and Blake (2 years old).

Since both of your children are under 16, they fall into the category where they may automatically qualify. However, I need to check a few more things to confirm their eligibility.

Let me check the next requirement for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to ask:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given that Alex is 10 and Blake is 2, I expect they're not working, but I need to confirm this for the assessment. This is a standard requirement to ensure children aren't in full-time employment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're just kids – Alex is in primary school and Blake is still a toddler!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's perfect! As expected for children their ages. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving certain benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?**

These would be benefits claimed in the child's own name (not your family's benefits, but specifically in the child's name). This is quite rare for young children, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're too young for that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to ask:

**Are either Alex or Blake in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Given their ages (10 and 2), this is very unlikely, but I need to confirm as different types of apprenticeships can affect eligibility depending on where you live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. They're far too young for apprenticeships!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

1. **Do you normally live in the UK?** (This is the most common situation)

OR any of these special circumstances:

2. Have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. Have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. Have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. Have pre-settled status with sufficient financial resources (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. Have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. Have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. Living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you meet any of these criteria?** The simplest one is: do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since you're living abroad, I need to check if you fall into one of the special circumstances that would still allow you to claim Child Benefit.

**Are you living abroad in any of these qualifying situations:**

1. **Are you a Crown servant** (working for the UK government overseas, such as in the diplomatic service or armed forces)?

2. **Are you living in certain qualifying countries** where UK Child Benefit can still be claimed?

OR

3. **Do you have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme** and meet any of the working, studying, or financial resource requirements I mentioned earlier?

Please let me know if any of these apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply to me. I'm just living abroad with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Based on the information you've provided, I need to check what this means for your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that based on the assessment, **you are not eligible for UK Child Benefit** for Alex and Blake at this time.

**The reason:** You do not meet the UK residency requirements.

**Why this matters:** To claim Child Benefit, you must either:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet one of the special circumstances such as:
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions)
  - Working abroad as a Crown servant
  - Living in certain qualifying countries

Since you're living abroad with your family and none of the special circumstances apply, you unfortunately don't qualify for Child Benefit.

**What you might consider:**
- If you move back to the UK and normally reside there, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that time
- Check if the country where you're currently living has any child support benefits available
- If your circumstances change (for example, if you return to the UK), you can apply then

Is there anything else about Child Benefit or your situation that you'd like me to clarify?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
I understand. Thank you for explaining that to me. It's disappointing, but at least now I know where I stand. I appreciate your help in working through this.

<function_calls>
<invoke name="exit_loop">
</invoke>
</function_calls>

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're very welcome, and I completely understand your disappointment. It's always better to know for certain, and I'm glad I could help clarify your situation.

Let me formally record the outcome of this assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Just to summarize for your records:

**Assessment Complete:**
- **Alex (age 10):** Not eligible - due to residency requirements
- **Blake (age 2):** Not eligible - due to residency requirements

Both children would otherwise qualify based on their age and circumstances, but the UK residency requirement means you cannot claim at this time. Should you return to live in the UK in the future, you would be able to apply then.

If you have any other questions in the future or if your circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out. Best of luck to you and your family!

reasonings: Alex (age 10) meets the child qualifying criteria as they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and do not fall under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake (age 2) meets the child qualifying criteria as they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and do not fall under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two qualifying children (Alex, 10, and Blake, 2) who both meet all child-related eligibility criteria. However, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. They are living abroad and do not qualify under any special circumstances such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in certain qualifying countries. UK residency is a fundamental requirement for Child Benefit eligibility.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Alex.

Blake (age 2) meets the child qualifying criteria as they are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they are living abroad and do not fall under any of the special circumstances (Crown servant, settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries). Therefore, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.

However, the claimant is ineligible for Child Benefit because they do not meet the UK residency requirements. They are living abroad and do not qualify under any special circumstances such as being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in certain qualifying countries.
